paragraphs.body: “Something you’re fascinated by, Obsessed with, or Wondering About”

In the article, “The Art of Bowling”, by John Kafalas, the author emphasizes the challenge and fascination of bowling. He transcends his view by giving many examples of beautiful moments, challenging ideas, and fascinating outcomes. This article takes the view of a fifty year old bowler, who loves what they do. First, Kafalas declares that in order to be a great bowler, you have to want to be good in order to be good. This idea stuck with me not only in bowling, but in almost every real world situation. Next, Kafalas disputes that you always have to lose something in order to win. Whether it’s time, friendship, or even other games, real winners have come back from losses. In terms of winning, Kafalas presents the idea of losing first because it inspires you to work even harder to win the next time you have an opportunity. Finally, Kafalas argues that the best winners face controversy on the way to victory. In his example, he faces terrible arm and shoulder stiffness, yet he was still able to walk out of the Easter Classic triumphant at 50 years old. This article resonates with me a lot because I love bowling, and it also presents common challenges that people face and how they overcome them.

annotations.text: This idea stuck with me not only in bowling, but in almost every real world situation.
annotations.comment: Remember that there's no "I say" in a summary. Not yet. This section should be all "They say."

annotations.text: Kafalas disputes that you always have to lose something in order to win.
annotations.comment: I don't quite understand this statement, but I'm interested!
remember that not all signal verbs mean the same thing. Is this the "right" word?

paragraphs.body: Although Kafalas heavily emphasizes his love for bowling in his article, “The Art of Bowling,” Kafalas tends to lean toward other goals and ideas that resonate to him. Instead, Kafalas emphasizes many lessons he learned along the way. Kafalas himself writes,”It’s not that winning isn’t important, but generally, my best takeaways are from losses,”(Kafalas 7). In other words, Kafalas thinks you have your best wins after your losses. This idea resonates to me especially because before my bowling season started, my soccer team suffered a heartbreaking loss in our county final, ending our season, and all of our high school soccer careers. But, this loss, like Kafalas, was only fuel under the fire to work even harder to win the next time I have a chance. Instead of grieving about the soccer season, myself and a few of my teammates prepared ourselves for a successful bowling season , making sure we came out of every match, practice, or meeting with a smile on our face knowing that we are winners. To look at a bigger example of a situation like this, the greatest basketball player of all time, Michael Jordan, was cut from his high school basketball team. Although he was pissed off, he worked his tail off in order to come back a stronger, more mature, and better basketball player than he was the last time he tried out. In doing so, his hard work became routine and he was soon one of the top recruited high school basketball players around the country. To paint a picture in the audience's mind, imagine a piece of paper in front of you with the word “comeback” on it. Now think about that word. How does it relate to you? What’s your most prominent comeback story? What causes people to come back?

But, this loss, like Kafalas, was only fuel under the fire to work even harder to win the next time I have a chance.

paragraphs.body: In addition to Kafalas’ first point about winning, he proposes that you have to want to be good in order to be good. After taking a deeper thought into this idea, it has many more sides to it than I expected. While taking a step back from the sports world, we see many people in their fields doing jobs, activities, and performances not because they have to but because they want to. “You’ll get almost as good as you want to! I love that, because it means there’s no limit to how much you can improve, if you just keep raising the bar,” (Kafalas 2). Kafalas in saying this, emphasizes that the only way to improve is to put the work in and to want to improve. Along with his last idea, I relate to this idea a lot because over the past few months I’ve developed a sense of anxiety, whether it's about the future, school, friends at home, or even just having anxiety itself. But in response, it was my best interest to seek the most amount of help as possible in order to make myself a better person. Whether it’s slowing down to take a deep breath, starting a new routine by going to the gym and taking walks, or seeing professionals to talk to about what's going on, I want to be good at shadowing my anxiety and I’m almost as good as I want to be at it. With both of these ideas in mind, they lead into the biggest picture of what Kafalas is declaring in his article, no one is perfect. Like Kafalas states, we all face adversity in our separate crafts and often need to rely on each other. One thing people should think about is that winners have lost as well and it's now in your hands whether you’ll work hard enough to be in their position. The next time I lose or see someone win, I’ll try to take a step back and look at the big picture, because although it might be tough in the moment, it makes you tough in the next moment. The next time you face a problem, it’s important to envision yourself coming out victorious the next time and challenge yourself throughout the whole process and make sure you succeed. In fact, I can use that exact mindset to write this essay. If the first time I write it isn’t a success, I can learn from the mistakes I made along the way, think about what I can improve on from the first time I wrote it, and envision myself writing an even better essay the next time I get the chance. John Kafalas’ article “The Art of Bowling” not only taught me lessons about how to improve myself as a bowler, but especially taught me to be a better thinker when times get hard. Although it’s such a short article, it really hits hard when you take a deep dive into it.
